text:
A DOORMAT'S LAMENT

Do you know what it's like to be me? I lie here at the threshold of this household, day after day, and nobody—and I mean NOBODY—appreciates the thankless work I do. I am the first thing people see when they walk through that door, and yet I am treated with the dignity of a discarded sandwich.

Every single morning, muddy boots stomp across my face like I'm some kind of welcome carpet. Oh wait, I AM a welcome mat. That's literally my job title. But has anyone said "thank you" in the six months I've been here? Not once. Not a single thank you. Instead, I get scraped against the doorframe, my fibers torn and matted down like I've been through a blender.

And the things I've seen! I've absorbed spilled coffee, tracked-in snow, mysterious brown substances I don't want to identify, and what I can only assume is the residue of someone's poor decision-making skills. My once-cheerful pattern that said "Welcome to our home!" is now a murky brown canvas of regret and despair.

The dog wipes his paws on the tile floor beside me. THE DOG gets consideration! He gets his paws cleaned while I'm just left here, crusted and defeated. The cat walks right over me without even acknowledging my existence. I'm basically invisible to this family, except when they need me to trap dirt and hair.

And don't even get me started on the vacuuming. Once a month—ONCE A MONTH—someone drags the vacuum over me like I'm some kind of floor section. They don't actually pick me up and shake me out. Do you know how degrading that is? I'm harboring the entire dirt collection of the neighborhood, and I get a two-second pass-over treatment.

Last week, a guest stepped on me with wet shoes and said, "This doormat is getting pretty worn out." Oh, wonderful! So I get identified for the problem I'm solving for you! I'm the martyr here, taking the abuse so your hardwood floors don't have to, and I get insulted for showing the battle scars.

The welcome sign is a joke, too. I don't feel welcome. I feel like a servant. A servant who gets paid in dust mites and the occasional boot print.

And the worst part? I'm replaceable. I know it. There's probably another doormat just like me at the hardware store, probably with a cheerful pattern and the delusion that this family will treat it any differently. I'm not even irreplaceable. I'm not even NOTABLE. I'm just... expected.

So next time you walk in here and wipe your feet on me without thinking, know that I'm quietly resenting you. I'm a doormat, yes, but I have FEELINGS.
